topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. Professor Albright was leaving. It was a sterile announcement, couched in polite administrative language, but the words swam before my eyes, blurring the screen. I stared at the subject line, 'Faculty Transition,' and felt a sudden, cold knot tighten in my chest. My hands began to tremble, the cheap plastic of my mouse feeling slick under my grip. I reread the message, searching for some hidden caveat, a sign that this wasn't real, but there was nothing but the finality of it. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on linoleum echoing too loudly in the otherwise silent library annex. I walked to the window, watching the autumn leaves spiral down, each one seeming to mock the stability I had just been promised. My throat felt thick, constricted, as if I couldn't draw a full breath.

[story 2]

"So, he's actually going?" I managed to croak out, the sound barely audible above the hum of the ventilation system. My voice sounded alien, reedy. I gripped the worn edges of my thesis draft, the paper suddenly feeling heavy and accusatory. Every line, every carefully constructed argument, seemed to crumble under the weight of this news. I paced the small confines of my dorm room, my footsteps quick and uneven. I stopped by my desk, running a hand over my face, feeling a flush creep up my neck. My vision tunneled slightly. Why now? After months of shared late nights, shared intellectual battles, this sudden void felt catastrophic. I sank onto my bed, pulling my knees to my chest, and just let the quiet wash over me, a heavy, suffocating blanket.

[story 3]

I kept refreshing my email, a frantic, repetitive motion. Each new notification brought a fresh wave of sinking feeling. Albright’s departure meant a complete realignment of my academic path, a sudden, terrifying lack of navigational structure. I stood by the whiteboard in the seminar room, tracing the outline of my proposed research topic with a marker, but the lines looked meaningless now, disconnected from the mentor who had helped forge them. A tightness seized my jaw; I bit down hard enough to taste copper. I started pacing the perimeter of the room, my gaze darting nervously toward the door, as if expecting the news to burst in. The air in the room felt thin, insufficient, and I felt utterly unmoored.
